---
title: "Clionaidae Thesis Analysis"
author: "Spencer Parr"
date: "Updated 2026-07-08"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: hide
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  out.width = "100%",
  results = "asis"
)

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(readr)
  library(dplyr)
  library(tidyr)
  library(stringr)
  library(knitr)
  library(scales)
})

safe_read <- function(path) {
  if (file.exists(path)) {
    readr::read_csv(path, show_col_types = FALSE)
  } else {
    tibble(.missing_file = path)
  }
}

show_table <- function(dat, n = 10, digits = 3, caption = NULL) {
  if (nrow(dat) == 0 || ".missing_file" %in% names(dat)) {
    missing <- if (".missing_file" %in% names(dat)) unique(dat$.missing_file) else "requested table"
    cat(paste0("<p class='missing'>Table not found: <code>", missing, "</code></p>"))
  } else {
    cat(knitr::kable(
      head(dat, n),
      format = "html",
      digits = digits,
      caption = caption,
      table.attr = "class='table table-striped table-condensed'"
    ))
  }
}

include_if_exists <- function(path, alt = path) {
  if (file.exists(path)) {
    knitr::include_graphics(path)
  } else {
    cat(paste0("<p class='missing'>Figure not found: <code>", path, "</code></p>"))
  }
}

fmt_p <- function(x) {
  ifelse(is.na(x), NA_character_, ifelse(x < 0.001, "<0.001", sprintf("%.3f", x)))
}

period_dat <- safe_read("poster_disturbance_period_data.csv")
period_tests <- safe_read("Table_Pooled_Step_Tests.csv")
depth_summary <- safe_read("poster_depth_association_summary.csv")
video_summary <- safe_read("poster_cliona_depth_species_summary.csv")
ivlev_oar <- safe_read("poster_ivlev_oar_data.csv")
glm_lrt <- safe_read("results_outputs/Table_3_1_1_LRT_species_length.csv")
glm_length <- safe_read("results_outputs/Table_3_1_1_length_effect.csv")
glm_or <- safe_read("results_outputs/Table_3_1_1_species_ORs.csv")
morph_prev <- safe_read("results_outputs/Table_3_1_2_morphology_prevalence.csv")
site_quasi <- safe_read("results_outputs/Table_3_2_1_site_quasi_aod.csv")
pca_var <- safe_read("results_outputs/Table_3_2_2_PCA_variance.csv")
pca_load <- safe_read("results_outputs/Table_3_2_2_PCA_loadings.csv")
depth_or <- safe_read("results_outputs/Table_3_2_2_depth_OR_by_species.csv")
depth_int <- safe_read("results_outputs/Table_3_2_2_species_depth_interaction_test.csv")
lm_depth <- safe_read("results_outputs/Table_3_2_2_lm_meanprev_depth_abundance_tidy.csv")
dharma <- safe_read("DHARMa_results_shallow.csv")

master_map <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Step, ~Source_file, ~Website_section, ~Purpose,
  "A", "plot_cliona_depth_abundance.R", "Video surveys", "Photo-confirmed sponge observations by depth, sponge species, and coral status.",
  "B", "detection_probability.R", "Data workflow", "Builds the TCRMP colony-level Clionaidae detection/prevalence workflow.",
  "C", "Depth&TemporalStuff.R", "Depth and disturbance", "Depth-stratified prevalence, temporal trends, disturbance windows, and shallow-site robustness.",
  "D", "depthsitePCA.R", "Depth and site structure", "Tests whether site identity, depth, and distance explain site-level prevalence patterns.",
  "E", "Working_PCA.R", "PCA and diagnostics", "Refined PCA/regression workflow with diagnostic and sensitivity checks.",
  "F", "StepChange_Bleaching.R", "Disturbance windows", "Tests step changes around hurricanes, SCTLD, and the 2023 bleaching period.",
  "G", "Correct_SPP_Length_GLM_2026.R", "Host species GLM", "Uses binomial GLMs to test host species and colony size effects on Clionaidae prevalence.",
  "H", "IVLEV_OAR_Fixed.R", "Host preference", "Calculates Ivlev electivity and observed:available ratios for host preference."
)

if (nrow(depth_summary) > 0 && !".missing_file" %in% names(depth_summary)) {
  shallow_prev <- depth_summary$prevalence_label[depth_summary$depth_bin == "<=12 m"]
  shallow_share <- percent(depth_summary$share_positive[depth_summary$depth_bin == "<=12 m"], accuracy = 0.1)
} else {
  shallow_prev <- "8.08%"
  shallow_share <- "79.3%"
}

if (nrow(period_dat) > 0 && !".missing_file" %in% names(period_dat)) {
  bleach_prev <- period_dat$pct_label[str_detect(period_dat$window, "Bleaching")]
  earlier_mean <- mean(period_dat$prevalence[period_dat$highlight == "Earlier periods"], na.rm = TRUE)
  earlier_label <- percent(earlier_mean, accuracy = 0.1)
} else {
  bleach_prev <- "3.21%"
  earlier_label <- "6.6%"
}

if (nrow(glm_length) > 0 && !".missing_file" %in% names(glm_length)) {
  length_5cm <- sprintf("%.1f%%", glm_length$pct_per_5cm[1])
} else {
  length_5cm <- "8.1%"
}
```

<style>
body {
  font-family: "Times New Roman", Times, serif;
  color: #111827;
  font-size: 18px;
  line-height: 1.5;
}
h1, h2, h3, h4, .nav, .tocify { font-family: "Times New Roman", Times, serif; }
.main-container { max-width: 1220px; }
.nav-tabs > li > a { font-size: 16px; padding: 10px 12px; }
.hero {
  border-left: 8px solid #2a9d8f;
  background: #f7faf9;
  padding: 1.15rem 1.4rem;
  margin: 1rem 0 1.35rem 0;
}
.hero h2 {
  margin-top: 0;
  font-size: clamp(30px, 3.2vw, 46px);
  font-weight: 700;
  line-height: 1.08;
  max-width: 1050px;
}
.hero p { max-width: 1080px; }
.grid-cards {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(260px, 1fr));
  gap: 18px;
  margin: 1rem 0 1.35rem 0;
}
.card {
  border: 1px solid #d7dee8;
  border-radius: 8px;
  padding: 16px 18px;
  background: #ffffff;
  min-width: 0;
  overflow-wrap: anywhere;
}
.card .big {
  font-size: clamp(32px, 3.4vw, 44px);
  font-weight: 700;
  color: #1f2937;
  line-height: 1.0;
  white-space: normal;
}
.card .label {
  color: #475569;
  font-size: 18px;
  line-height: 1.2;
  margin-top: 9px;
  white-space: normal;
}
.business-card {
  border: 1px solid #d7dee8;
  border-radius: 10px;
  background: #ffffff;
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: minmax(0, 1.35fr) minmax(220px, 0.65fr);
  gap: 28px;
  align-items: center;
  padding: 30px;
  margin: 1rem 0 1.5rem 0;
  box-shadow: 0 8px 24px rgba(15, 23, 42, 0.06);
}
.business-card h2 {
  font-size: clamp(36px, 4vw, 58px);
  line-height: 1;
  margin: 0 0 10px 0;
}
.business-card .role {
  font-size: 24px;
  font-weight: 700;
  color: #0f766e;
  margin-bottom: 12px;
}
.business-card .credential,
.business-card .org {
  font-size: 20px;
  color: #334155;
  margin: 6px 0;
}
.contact-list {
  margin-top: 18px;
  padding-top: 14px;
  border-top: 1px solid #e5e7eb;
  font-size: 20px;
}
.contact-list div { margin: 4px 0; }
.cwori-logo {
  width: 100%;
  max-width: 310px;
  display: block;
  margin-left: auto;
  border: none;
}
.profile-note {
  color: #475569;
  font-size: 17px;
  margin-top: 14px;
}
.note, .interpretation {
  border-left: 5px solid #d64545;
  background: #fff7f7;
  padding: 0.85rem 1rem;
  margin: 1rem 0;
}
.method {
  border-left: 5px solid #4e79a7;
  background: #f4f8fc;
  padding: 0.85rem 1rem;
  margin: 1rem 0;
}
.missing { color: #8a341f; font-weight: 600; }
table { width: 100%; font-size: 15px; }
th { background: #eef2f7; }
caption { color: #111827; font-weight: 700; }
img { border: 1px solid #e5e7eb; max-width: 100%; height: auto; }
@media (max-width: 760px) {
  .business-card { grid-template-columns: 1fr; padding: 22px; }
  .cwori-logo { margin: 0; max-width: 260px; }
  .nav-tabs > li > a { font-size: 15px; padding: 8px 10px; }
}
</style>

# Spencer Parr Thesis Analysis {.tabset .tabset-fade}

## Contact

<div class="business-card">
  <div>
    <h2>Spencer Wolf Parr</h2>
    <div class="role">Coral Conservation Technician</div>
    <div class="org">CWORI - Coral World Ocean and Reef Initiative</div>
    <div class="credential">Master's in Marine and Environmental Science</div>
    <div class="credential">University of the Virgin Islands (UVI)</div>
    <div class="contact-list">
      <div><strong>Phone:</strong> 340-642-1974</div>
      <div><strong>Email:</strong> <a href="mailto:spencer@cwori.org">spencer@cwori.org</a></div>
    </div>
    <div class="profile-note">Thesis website for Clionaidae prevalence, host preference, reef depth, and disturbance analyses in the U.S. Virgin Islands.</div>
  </div>
  <div>
    <img class="cwori-logo" src="CWORI-logo.webp" alt="CWORI logo">
  </div>
</div>

## References

1. Chaves-Fonnegra, A., Riegl, B., Zea, S., Lopez, J. V., Smith, T., Brandt, M., & Gilliam, D. S. (2018). Bleaching events regulate shifts from corals to excavating sponges in algae-dominated reefs. *Global Change Biology, 24*(2), 773-785. https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13962

2. Hughes, T. P., Baird, A. H., Bellwood, D. R., Card, M., Connolly, S. R., Folke, C., Grosberg, R., Hoegh-Guldberg, O., Jackson, J. B. C., Kleypas, J., Lough, J. M., Marshall, P., Nystrom, M., Palumbi, S. R., Pandolfi, J. M., Rosen, B., & Roughgarden, J. (2003). Climate change, human impacts, and the resilience of coral reefs. *Science, New Series, 301*(5635), 929-933.

3. Hughes, T. P., Graham, N. A. J., Jackson, J. B. C., Mumby, P. J., & Steneck, R. S. (2010). Rising to the challenge of sustaining coral reef resilience. *Trends in Ecology & Evolution, 25*(11), 633-642. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tree.2010.07.011

4. Lopez-Victoria, M., Zea, S., & Weil, E. (2006). Competition for space between encrusting excavating Caribbean sponges and other coral reef organisms. *Marine Ecology Progress Series, 312*, 113-121. https://doi.org/10.3354/meps312113

5. Perry, C. T., Murphy, G. N., Kench, P. S., Smithers, S. G., Edinger, E. N., Steneck, R. S., & Mumby, P. J. (2013). Caribbean-wide decline in carbonate production threatens coral reef growth. *Nature Communications, 4*(1), 1402. https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms2409

6. Schonberg, C. H. L. (2008). A history of sponge erosion: From past myths and hypotheses to recent approaches. In M. Wisshak & L. Tapanila (Eds.), *Current Developments in Bioerosion* (pp. 165-202). Springer Berlin Heidelberg. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-540-77598-0_9

7. Wisshak, M., Schonberg, C. H. L., Form, A., & Freiwald, A. (2012). Ocean acidification accelerates reef bioerosion. *PLoS ONE, 7*(9), e45124. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0045124

## Overview

<div class="hero">
<h2>Clionaidae prevalence is structured by host identity, reef depth, and recent disturbance.</h2>
<p>This website is a rebuilt thesis analysis report for the TCRMP coral-health and benthic datasets. It follows the analysis map in <code>Combined_Analysis_Master.R</code> and presents the current outputs as figures, tables, and short interpretations.</p>
</div>

<div class="grid-cards">
  <div class="card"><div class="big">2012-2024</div><div class="label">Primary analysis window</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="big">`r shallow_prev`</div><div class="label">Prevalence at very shallow sites (<=12 m)</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="big">`r shallow_share`</div><div class="label">Positive focal colonies found at <=12 m sites</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="big">`r bleach_prev`</div><div class="label">Prevalence in the 2023-2024 bleaching window</div></div>
</div>

**Main story.** Clionaidae prevalence is not evenly distributed across reef space or host corals. The updated analyses show strong host-species differences, a clear decline with depth, and a sharp reduction in prevalence after the 2023 bleaching period.

**How to read this report.** Each tab has three pieces: what the analysis asks, what model or metric was used, and what the result means biologically. Code is hidden by default, but the source files are listed in the analysis map tab.

## Site Map

<div class="method">
<strong>What this map shows.</strong> This map gives the spatial context for the TCRMP reef sites used throughout the thesis analyses. It is useful for interpreting the depth, reef-complex, and site-identity results because those patterns are geographically structured across the U.S. Virgin Islands.
</div>

```{r site-map-figure, fig.cap="TCRMP reef-site map used for thesis spatial context."}
include_if_exists("SiteMap.png")
```

[Open the full-size site map PDF](SiteMap.pdf)

## Data Workflow

<div class="method">
<strong>What this workflow does.</strong> The data workflow starts from the TCRMP coral-health colony, condition, interaction, and site files. Clionaidae-positive colonies are identified from interaction codes including <code>CLIO</code>, <code>CLSP</code>, and <code>BOSP</code>. The core analyses focus on annual/post-bleaching permanent transect records collected with intercept or belt methods, then summarize prevalence as positive colonies divided by total colonies.
</div>

**Why this matters.** The response variable in most models is binary at the colony level: a coral colony is either Clionaidae-positive or not. That structure is why the major statistical tests use binomial GLMs or grouped binomial counts.

```{r master-map-short}
show_table(master_map[, c("Step", "Source_file", "Purpose")], n = 8, caption = "Analysis files used to rebuild this website")
```

## Host Preference

<div class="method">
<strong>What Ivlev and OAR are used for.</strong> Ivlev electivity compares the proportional use of a coral host to its proportional availability. Values above 0 mean Clionaidae occurred on that host more often than expected from availability; values below 0 suggest avoidance. The observed:available ratio (OAR) shows the same idea as a ratio, where 1 is neutral, greater than 1 is over-represented, and less than 1 is under-represented.
</div>

```{r host-preference-figure, fig.cap="Poster-ready combined Ivlev electivity and observed:available ratio figure."}
include_if_exists("Poster_Ivlev_OAR_Combined.png")
```

<div class="interpretation">
<strong>Interpretation.</strong> Preference is strongest for several massive or stress-tolerant hosts, especially <em>Orbicella annularis</em> and <em>Siderastrea siderea</em>. Some high Ivlev/OAR values have very wide confidence intervals because those corals were rare or had few positive colonies, so the graph correctly treats those as uncertain rather than as strong biological evidence.
</div>

```{r ivlev-table}
ivlev_top <- ivlev_oar %>%
  filter(!(".missing_file" %in% names(.))) %>%
  arrange(desc(Ei)) %>%
  transmute(
    species = species_group,
    colonies = n_total,
    positives = k_total,
    Ei = round(Ei, 2),
    OAR = round(OAR, 2),
    OAR_95_CI = paste0(round(OAR_lo, 2), "-", round(OAR_hi, 2)),
    uncertainty = uncertainty_group
  )
show_table(ivlev_top, n = 12, caption = "Highest host preference estimates with uncertainty flags")
```

## Host Species GLM

<div class="method">
<strong>What the GLM is used for.</strong> The host-species GLM tests whether the probability that a colony is Clionaidae-positive changes with host species and colony length. Because the response is yes/no at the colony level, a binomial GLM is appropriate. The likelihood-ratio tests ask whether adding species, length, and species-by-length terms improves the model.
</div>

```{r glm-main-figure, fig.cap="Species-level prevalence summary from the updated GLM workflow."}
include_if_exists("Species_prevalence_barplot.png")
```

<div class="interpretation">
<strong>Interpretation.</strong> Host species identity strongly improves model fit, colony length also improves model fit, and the interaction term indicates that size effects are not identical across all host species. Holding species constant, the length effect corresponds to about `r length_5cm` higher odds of Clionaidae occurrence per 5 cm increase in colony length.
</div>

```{r glm-lrt-table}
show_table(glm_lrt, n = 3, caption = "Likelihood-ratio tests for host species and colony length GLMs")
```

```{r glm-length-table}
length_tbl <- glm_length %>%
  filter(!(".missing_file" %in% names(.))) %>%
  transmute(
    term,
    log_odds_estimate = estimate,
    p_value = p.value,
    odds_increase_per_cm_pct = pct_per_cm,
    odds_increase_per_5cm_pct = pct_per_5cm
  )
show_table(length_tbl, n = 1, caption = "Colony length effect from the additive species + length GLM")
```

```{r glm-or-table}
glm_or_top <- glm_or %>%
  filter(!(".missing_file" %in% names(.))) %>%
  arrange(desc(OR)) %>%
  mutate(
    OR = round(OR, 2),
    CI = paste0(round(OR_lo, 2), "-", round(OR_hi, 2)),
    p_FDR = fmt_p(p_fdr)
  ) %>%
  select(species, OR, CI, p_FDR)
show_table(glm_or_top, n = 12, caption = "Top adjusted host-species odds ratios from the GLM")
```

## Depth Signal

<div class="method">
<strong>What the depth analysis is used for.</strong> The depth analysis asks whether Clionaidae prevalence changes across site depth bins and whether very shallow reefs explain most of the positive colonies. This direct depth-bin summary is easier to interpret than a PCA for audiences who need the biological result quickly.
</div>

```{r depth-association-figure, fig.cap="Poster-ready depth association figure for focal coral hosts."}
include_if_exists("Poster_Depth_Association_Figure.png")
```

<div class="interpretation">
<strong>Interpretation.</strong> Prevalence is highest at <=12 m and drops strongly at deeper bins. Very shallow reefs represent about half of the focal-colony sampling effort, but they contain most Clionaidae-positive focal colonies. That makes depth the clearest spatial signal in the current analysis.
</div>

```{r depth-summary-table}
depth_tbl <- depth_summary %>%
  filter(!(".missing_file" %in% names(.))) %>%
  transmute(
    depth_bin,
    colonies,
    positives = clionaid_positive,
    prevalence = prevalence_label,
    share_all_colonies = percent(share_colonies, accuracy = 0.1),
    share_positive_colonies = percent(share_positive, accuracy = 0.1)
  )
show_table(depth_tbl, n = 3, caption = "Depth-bin prevalence and contribution to Clionaidae-positive colonies")
```

## PCA and Model Context

<div class="method">
<strong>What the depth GLM and PCA are used for.</strong> The grouped binomial GLMs test the depth association statistically using counts of positive and non-positive colonies. The PCA is exploratory: it shows how site depth, distance from shore, and mean prevalence align, but it is not the cleanest primary explanation for the poster or website.
</div>

```{r pca-figure, fig.cap="Exploratory PCA of site depth, distance from shore, and Clionaidae prevalence."}
include_if_exists("Poster_Clionaid_PCA.png")
```

<div class="interpretation">
<strong>Interpretation.</strong> The PCA is useful as a compact exploratory view of spatial gradients. PC1 captures most of the variation and mainly separates deeper/offshore structure from higher-prevalence shallow sites, but the direct depth-bin figure remains the clearest visual for communicating the depth result.
</div>

```{r depth-model-tables}
show_table(lm_depth, n = 5, caption = "Linear model of site mean prevalence against depth and coral abundance")
show_table(depth_int, n = 2, caption = "Species-by-depth interaction test from the grouped binomial GLM")
```

```{r pca-tables}
show_table(pca_var, n = 3, caption = "PCA variance explained")
show_table(pca_load, n = 3, caption = "PCA loadings for depth, distance, and mean prevalence")
```

## Disturbance Windows

<div class="method">
<strong>What the disturbance analysis is used for.</strong> This analysis pools shallow focal-host colonies by disturbance period: pre-2017, hurricanes 2017-2018, SCTLD 2019-2022, and bleaching 2023-2024. It tests whether prevalence changed across those windows and whether the 2023 bleaching period marks a step change.
</div>

```{r disturbance-figure, fig.cap="Poster-ready disturbance-period figure showing the post-2023 bleaching decline."}
include_if_exists("Poster_Disturbance_Signal.png")
```

<div class="interpretation">
<strong>Interpretation.</strong> Reef-wide shallow prevalence was stable near `r earlier_label` through the earlier windows, then fell to `r bleach_prev` during the 2023-2024 bleaching window. The pairwise tests show the bleaching window is significantly lower than earlier periods after FDR adjustment.
</div>

```{r disturbance-tables}
period_tbl <- period_dat %>%
  filter(!(".missing_file" %in% names(.))) %>%
  select(window, n_colonies, n_cliona, `Prevalence (%)`, `95% CI`)
show_table(period_tbl, n = 4, caption = "Pooled prevalence by disturbance window")
show_table(period_tests, n = 6, caption = "Step-change tests among disturbance windows")
```

## Video Surveys

<div class="method">
<strong>What the video-survey figure is used for.</strong> This is supporting evidence, not the main TCRMP colony analysis. It summarizes photo-confirmed 2019 Clionaidae observations by site depth, sponge species, and coral status. It is useful because it independently corroborates the shallow-water pattern.
</div>

```{r video-survey-figure, fig.cap="Photo-confirmed 2019 video-survey observations by depth, Clionaidae species, and coral status."}
include_if_exists("Poster_Cliona_Depth_Abundance.png")
```

<div class="interpretation">
<strong>Interpretation.</strong> Most observations are concentrated above 20 m, especially for <em>C. delitrix</em>. The video data should be described as corroborating evidence because it comes from the photo/video survey dataset rather than the full TCRMP colony-level denominator used for prevalence models.
</div>

```{r video-summary-table}
show_table(video_summary, n = 6, caption = "Video-survey observation counts and mean depth by Clionaidae species")
```

## Morphology and Site Structure

<div class="method">
<strong>What these summaries are used for.</strong> Morphology summaries ask whether Clionaidae occurrence is concentrated on growth forms that may differ in skeletal structure or exposure. Site-structure models ask whether spatial site identity explains additional variation beyond broad depth and distance gradients.
</div>

```{r morphology-table}
morph_tbl <- morph_prev %>%
  filter(!(".missing_file" %in% names(.))) %>%
  mutate(
    prevalence_pct = percent(prevalence, accuracy = 0.1),
    CI = paste0(percent(lo, accuracy = 0.1), "-", percent(hi, accuracy = 0.1))
  ) %>%
  select(morph, N, K, prevalence_pct, CI)
show_table(morph_tbl, n = 8, caption = "Clionaidae prevalence by coral morphology")
```

```{r site-structure-table}
show_table(site_quasi, n = 5, caption = "Grouped binomial site model: species and location effects")
```

<div class="interpretation">
<strong>Interpretation.</strong> Morphology is useful descriptively, but host species and site identity remain important sources of variation. The site model shows that local reef identity explains additional prevalence structure, which means depth is a major signal but not the only ecological driver.
</div>

## Diagnostics and Sensitivity

<div class="method">
<strong>What diagnostics are used for.</strong> Diagnostics check whether the binomial model assumptions are reasonable and whether the main conclusions survive alternative filters, such as different shallow-depth cutoffs or sparse-cell removal.
</div>

```{r dharma-figure, fig.cap="DHARMa diagnostic output for the shallow prevalence model."}
include_if_exists("DHARMa_results_shallow.png")
```

```{r dharma-table}
show_table(dharma, n = 10, caption = "DHARMa diagnostic tests for the shallow model")
```

```{r sensitivity-figure, fig.cap="Sensitivity of annual odds ratio to shallow-depth cutoff."}
include_if_exists("Fig_S2_OR_by_depth.png")
```

<div class="interpretation">
<strong>Interpretation.</strong> These checks are included so the thesis results are not just visually persuasive but statistically defensible. When a model shows overdispersion or sparse-cell uncertainty, the report treats that result cautiously and leans on robustness checks and direct summaries.
</div>

## Analysis Map

This tab connects the website back to the master runner. The site is organized around the same eight analysis blocks in <code>Combined_Analysis_Master.R</code>.

```{r full-master-map}
show_table(master_map, n = 8, caption = "Combined analysis master map")
```

To rerun the full analysis pipeline from R, use:

```{r rerun-master, eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE}
source("Combined_Analysis_Master.R")
```

For website updates, the safest workflow is:

1. Run or update the relevant analysis script.
2. Confirm the figure/table output changed.
3. Render <code>index.Rmd</code>.
4. Review <code>index.html</code> in a browser.
